visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens do not need a visa or residence permit to enter Slovenia. They require only a valid identity card or passport. Freedom of movement applies.

UK nationals (British citizens) are visa-free for Schengen area including Slovenia for up to 90 days in any 180-day period following Brexit, under the post-Brexit arrangement.

visa_policy.notes: Slovenia is a Schengen Area member state and applies EU Regulation 2018/1806 uniformly. Visa-free list is Annex II of that Regulation. VANUATU CORRECTION (validated June 2026): Vanuatu nationals are NO LONGER visa-free. EU Regulation 2025/11 (adopted 19 December 2024, effective 3 February 2025) permanently transferred Vanuatu from Annex II to Annex I (visa required). Annex I (visa required) now includes Vanuatu in addition to: Afghanistan, Armenia, Angola, Azerbaijan, Bangladesh, Burkina Faso, Bahrain, Burundi, Benin, Bolivia, Bhutan, Botswana, Belarus, Belize, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Central African Republic, Congo, Cote d'Ivoire, Cameroon, China, Cuba, Cape Verde, Djibouti, Dominican Republic, Algeria, Ecuador, Egypt, Eritrea, Eswatini, Ethiopia, Fiji, Gabon, Ghana, The Gambia, Guinea, Equatorial Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Guyana, Haiti, Indonesia, India, Iraq, Iran, Jamaica, Jordan, Kenya, Kyrgyzstan, Cambodia, Comoros, North Korea, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Laos, Lebanon, Sri Lanka, Liberia, Lesotho, Libya, Morocco, Madagascar, Mali, Myanmar/Burma, Mongolia, Mauritania, Maldives, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Niger, Nigeria, Nepal, Oman, Papua New Guinea, Philippines, Pakistan, Qatar, Russia (suspended), Rwanda, Saudi Arabia, Sudan, Sierra Leone, Senegal, Somalia, Suriname, South Sudan, Sao Tome and Principe, Syria, Chad, Togo, Thailand, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Tunisia, Turkey, Tanzania, Uganda, Uzbekistan, Vietnam, Yemen, South Africa, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Palestinian Authority. GEORGIA DIPLOMATIC PASSPORT SUSPENSION NOTE (validated June 2026): Georgia ordinary biometric passport holders remain visa-free under Annex II (unchanged). However, the European Commission suspended visa-free travel for Georgian holders of DIPLOMATIC, SERVICE, and OFFICIAL passports, effective 6 March 2026 until 6 March 2027. This affects only diplomatic/service/official Georgian passport holders; ordinary passport holders are unaffected. Source: Council press release 27 January 2025 (consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/01/27/georgia-council-suspends-visa-free-travel-for-diplomats-and-officials/). ETIAS NOTE: As of June 2026 ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) has not yet launched; it is scheduled for Q4 2026. When launched, visa-exempt nationals (e.g. USA, CAN, AUS, JPN, GBR) will need to obtain an ETIAS pre-travel authorisation (EUR 20, valid 3 years or until passport expiry, whichever is sooner) before arriving in Slovenia. ENTRY/EXIT SYSTEM (EES): EES had a gradual rollout beginning 12 October 2025 and became fully operational across all Schengen countries on 10 April 2026. No advance registration required; biometric data collected at border crossing on arrival. Source: home-affairs.ec.europa.eu (April 2026). Slovenia has no national e-visa system separate from the Schengen framework. Short-stay Schengen visa costs EUR 90 for adults, EUR 45 for children aged 6-12; processed normally within 15 days (up to 45 days).

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a short-stay visa (Type C) issued by any Schengen country may, during the visa validity period, enter the Republic of Slovenia and stay in its territory, provided that the total length of their stay in Schengen countries does not exceed 90 days in any 180-day period, or until the expiry of the visa, whichever is sooner. Applies to nationalities that would otherwise require a visa for Slovenia (Annex I countries).

Holders of a long-stay visa (Type D) issued by any Schengen country may enter the Republic of Slovenia with this visa and a valid travel document and stay in its territory, provided that the total length of their stay in Schengen countries, except in the territory of the Schengen country that issued the long-stay visa, does not exceed 90 days in any 180-day period, or until the expiry of the long-stay visa, whichever is sooner. Applies to nationalities that would otherwise require a visa for Slovenia.

Holders of a residence permit issued by any Schengen country may enter the Republic of Slovenia with this residence permit and a valid travel document and stay in its territory, provided that the total length of their stay in Schengen countries, except in the territory of the Schengen country that issued the residence permit, does not exceed 90 days in any 180-day period, or until the expiry of the residence permit, whichever is sooner. Applies to nationalities that would otherwise require a visa for Slovenia.

Holders of a valid Chinese diplomatic passport are visa-free for short stays in Slovenia (and all Schengen area states) under the EU-China short-stay visa waiver agreement for holders of diplomatic passports. Maximum 90 days in any 180-day period. Agreement entered into force 1 January 2017.

Holders of Brazilian diplomatic, service or official passports are visa-free for short stays in Slovenia (and all Schengen area states) under the EU-Brazil short-stay visa waiver agreement for holders of diplomatic, service or official passports. Agreement entered into force 1 April 2011; amended agreement published OJ L 179, 14.7.2023.

Holders of Mongolian diplomatic and service passports are visa-free for Slovenia under a bilateral visa waiver agreement signed between Slovenia and Mongolia on 15 October 2020, concluded under EU Regulation allowing Member States to define exemptions from visa requirements for holders of diplomatic, service and other status passports. Specific stay duration not published in the official announcement.

Holders of Indian diplomatic passports do not need a short-stay visa to enter Slovenia. Ordinary Indian passport holders (including OCI cardholders) require a Schengen visa. Source: official gov.si country page for India.

cbi.notes: Slovenia does not offer a citizenship-by-investment program. No legal framework exists to obtain Slovenian citizenship solely through investment. Citizenship is available only through naturalisation after 10 years of total legal residence (with the last 5 years continuous). Confirmed by absence of any official program on gov.si or mnz.gov.si.

programs.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Business Investors / Company Director
programs.notes: Non-EU nationals may obtain a work permit (and thus residence) by establishing or investing in a Slovenian limited liability company (d.o.o.). For companies not yet registered for 6 months, proof of investment of at least EUR 30,000 for company start-up is required to obtain a work permit for a foreign representative. The permit is issued for up to 2 years and is renewable. After 5 years of continuous legal residence, permanent residency is available; citizenship via naturalisation after 10 years total. There is no dedicated 'golden visa' or standalone investor visa program - residence flows from business activity, not passive investment.

programs.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Digital Nomads
programs.eligibility: Non-EU/EEA nationals who work remotely for a company or clients registered outside Slovenia, or are self-employed abroad. Must have monthly funds of at least twice the average monthly net salary in Slovenia (approximately EUR 3,200/month as of early 2026). Must hold valid health insurance (min EUR 30,000 coverage) and have a clean criminal record.
programs.notes: Launched 21 November 2025. Permit issued for up to one year; cannot be extended. Reapplication possible 6 months after expiry. Alternative path: holders may switch to other residence permit categories at any time during permit validity. Family members may join immediately without duration restrictions. Applications submitted at Slovenian diplomatic missions abroad or at any administrative unit in Slovenia.

programs.name: EU Blue Card
programs.eligibility: Non-EU nationals with a valid work contract or binding job offer for highly qualified employment (at least 6 months under 2025 amendments) paying at least 1.5x the average annual gross salary in Slovenia. Requires higher professional qualifications (university level) or, for IT professionals, proven competence via certificates or expert opinion.
programs.processing_time: 30 days (up to 60 days for complex cases)
programs.notes: Issued for the duration of the employment contract plus 3 months, maximum 2 years; renewable for up to 3 years. Labour market test applies. Initial application fee EUR 70-102; residence permit card EUR 15.47. 2025 amendments loosened requirements: contract minimum reduced from 1 year to 6 months, and IT professionals may qualify without academic degrees.

programs.name: Single Permit (Combined Residence and Work Permit)
programs.eligibility: Third-country nationals seeking employment in Slovenia. Employer must demonstrate no suitable EU/Slovenian worker was available (labour market test).
programs.notes: Combines residence and work permit into a single administrative procedure. Available for various employment categories including seasonal workers and intra-corporate transferees.

programs.name: Work Permit for Foreign Company Representative / Director
programs.eligibility: Non-EU nationals who are directors or procurators of a Slovenian company (d.o.o. or other entity). Companies with 10 or fewer workers may obtain no more than one such permit.
programs.notes: For companies registered less than 6 months: minimum investment of EUR 30,000 for company start-up required. Valid for up to 2 years; renewable. Application fee approximately EUR 90.

validation_notes: CORRECTION (June 2026 re-validation): Vanuatu (VUT) removed from visa_policy.visa_free. EU Regulation 2025/11 (adopted 19 December 2024) permanently transferred Vanuatu from Annex II to Annex I effective 3 February 2025. Prior record incorrectly listed Vanuatu as visa-free with only a suspension note. Vanuatu nationals now require a Schengen visa to enter Slovenia. Source: consilium.europa.eu press release 12 Dec 2024; nyidanmark.dk (Danish immigration authority confirming permanent status); EUR-Lex OJ L 202402059.

CORRECTION (June 2026 re-validation): Kosovo source_url updated from the 'signing' announcement (April 2023) to the 'entry into force' announcement (January 2024) confirming the effective date of 1 January 2024. Source: home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/news/visa-free-travel-kosovo-citizens-eu-2024-01-03_en.
ADDITION (June 2026 re-validation): visa_policy.notes updated with ETIAS status (not yet launched; planned Q4 2026) and EES (launched 12 October 2025) for completeness.

CLARIFICATION (June 2026 re-validation): Digital Nomad permit income threshold of EUR 3,200/month confirmed by multiple secondary sources consistent with the official gov.si formulation of 'twice the average monthly net salary'. The official_url confirmed as live and accurate.

No change to conditional_access entries: Schengen visa/RP credential-based access, Chinese diplomatic passport waiver, Brazilian diplomatic/service/official passport waiver, Mongolian diplomatic/service bilateral waiver, Indian diplomatic passport exemption - all remain as prior record.

RE-VALIDATION (2026-06-27): Full re-check against EUR-Lex consolidated Regulation 2018/1806 as of 03.02.2025 (CELEX:02018R1806-20250203) and gov.si entry page. No new changes to visa_free list required. Vanuatu removal (Reg. 2025/11, eff. 3 Feb 2025) re-confirmed. Kosovo visa-free status re-confirmed (Annex II with footnote noting 1 Jan 2024 effective date). Nicaragua remains in Annex II (visa-free for Schengen); note: Nicaragua imposing visa requirements on certain EU nationals for travel TO Nicaragua (outbound, February 2026) does not affect inbound policy for Nicaragua nationals entering Slovenia. ETIAS confirmed still not launched; official timeline now Q4 2026. EES operational since October 2025 confirmed. All 61 states plus special categories in Annex II cross-checked against current record - no additions or removals detected. Source: eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:02018R1806-20250203.

RE-VALIDATION (2026-06-29): Re-verification against official EU and Slovenia government sources. (1) EES STATUS CORRECTION: The notes previously stated 'EES began operations on 12 October 2025'. Corrected to reflect that EES had a gradual rollout from 12 October 2025 and became FULLY OPERATIONAL across all Schengen countries on 10 April 2026. Source: home-affairs.ec.europa.eu (April 2026 EC announcement). (2) GEORGIA DIPLOMATIC PASSPORT SUSPENSION ADDED to notes: The EU suspended visa-free travel for Georgian diplomatic, service, and official passport holders effective 6 March 2026 until 6 March 2027 (Council decision, January 2025). Georgia's ordinary biometric passport holders remain on Annex II visa-free list - NO change to visa_free entry for GEO. The suspension only affects diplomatic/service/official passports, which are at the conditional_access tier. (3) ETIAS: Re-confirmed scheduled for Q4 2026, EUR 20 fee, 3-year validity. No launch date yet announced. (4) Annex II visa-free list: Re-confirmed 61 countries as of June 2026. No new countries added or removed since the Feb 2025 consolidation (Reg. 2025/11 removed Vanuatu). No other Annex II changes detected. Sources: home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/news/main-differences-between-ees-and-etias-what-travellers-need-know-2026-04-28_en; consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/01/27/georgia-council-suspends-visa-free-travel-for-diplomats-and-officials/.
gaps: ETIAS exact launch date within Q4 2026 not yet officially announced as of June 2026.

Mongolia bilateral diplomatic/service passport waiver (signed 15 Oct 2020) - exact entry-into-force date and permitted stay duration not published in the official gov.si news release; only the signing date is confirmed.

Comprehensive list of all bilateral diplomatic/service passport visa waiver agreements signed by Slovenia could not be retrieved from mzz.gov.si (connection errors during validation). Only Mongolia is confirmed via an official gov.si news release; India diplomatic passport exemption confirmed via gov.si/en/countries/india page. Additional bilateral agreements may exist but are not verifiable from accessible official sources.
Digital Nomad permit exact processing time not stated in official gov.si source.

The official Slovenia MFA website (mzz.gov.si) was not directly accessible during re-validation (connection errors); visa policy data was sourced from the authoritative EU Regulation 2018/1806 (official EU source) which uniformly governs all Schengen states including Slovenia.

Annex II footnote countries (Dominica, Grenada, FSM, Kiribati, Marshall Islands, Nauru, Palau, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Timor-Leste, Tonga, Tuvalu, Peru): the original regulation footnote stated exemption applies 'from the date of entry into force of an agreement on visa exemption to be concluded with the EU'. EU bilateral agreements with these countries have been concluded (Council Decisions 2016/268–271 for Caribbean four; similar decisions for Pacific states) and appear to have entered into force (e.g. Grenada: OJ L 6, 9.1.2019). These countries are correctly listed in visa_free. Exact entry-into-force dates for each individual agreement were not confirmed from official sources in this validation cycle; the entries are preserved as-is per the preservation rule.
No official Slovenian-specific e-visa portal found; Slovenia applies standard Schengen visa procedures with no national e-visa scheme.

UK nationals' post-Brexit 90-day visa-free status for Schengen is referenced from the gov.si entry page; the specific mechanism (UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement) was not separately verified at source during this validation cycle.

Georgia (GEO) diplomatic/service/official passport suspension (eff. 6 March 2026 – 6 March 2027): The conditional_access section does not yet have a specific GEO diplomatic/service/official passport entry. Since the current suspension means GEO diplomatic/service/official passport holders NO LONGER have visa-free access (they now require a Schengen visa for the suspension period), adding a conditional_access entry for GEO diplomatic passports would be misleading without clearer post-suspension resolution. This gap is noted: the suspension runs until 6 March 2027; the GEO entry in visa_free (ordinary biometric passports) correctly remains. Source: consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/01/27/georgia-council-suspends-visa-free-travel-for-diplomats-and-officials/.

outbound_official.note: No official Slovenia government outbound visa-list page located. Slovenia's Ministry of Foreign and European Affairs (mzz.gov.si) and the e-government portal (e-uprava.gov.si) do not publish a consolidated list of countries where Slovenian ordinary-passport holders can travel without a prior consular visa. Official Slovenian government pages (e-uprava.gov.si/si/podrocja/potovanja-vizumi/dokumenti-vizumi/vizumski-postopki.html and e-uprava.gov.si/en/podrocja/travel/documents-and-visas/visa-procedures.html) direct citizens to consult individual embassy and consulate pages via the 'Predstavništva po svetu' (Representations Worldwide) directory at mzz.gov.si/si/predstavnistva_po_svetu/ - a per-embassy contact directory, not a compiled visa-free country list. The mzz.gov.si domain was unresponsive to direct fetch attempts. No origin-side official page analogous to India's mea.gov.in/vffin was found. Rely on destination-side data.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C)
visa_types.purpose: Allows third-country nationals to enter and stay in Slovenia (and the Schengen Area) for tourism, family visits, business meetings, or other short-term purposes. Valid for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Fee is EUR 90 for adults, EUR 45 for children aged 6-12 (approx USD 98/49 at current rates). Applications must be submitted in person at a Slovenian diplomatic mission or consular post at least 15 days before intended travel. Required documents include valid passport, application form, photo, travel medical insurance (min EUR 30,000 coverage), proof of accommodation, and proof of financial means. Normal processing is 15 days; may be extended to 45 days if additional examination is needed. Single-entry variants also available. As a Schengen member, a Type C visa issued by Slovenia is valid across all Schengen states.

visa_types.name: Airport Transit Visa (Type A)
visa_types.purpose: Required for certain nationalities to pass through the international transit zone of Slovenian airports without entering the Schengen Area.
visa_types.notes: Only required for nationals of specific countries listed in EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex I who are transiting through a Slovenian airport. Many nationalities are exempt. Fee follows standard Schengen visa fee schedule (EUR 90 for adults). Processing times align with standard Schengen procedures.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C) - Business
visa_types.purpose: Permits third-country nationals to travel to Slovenia for business meetings, conferences, trade fairs, contract negotiations, or other commercial activities not constituting employment.
visa_types.notes: No separate business visa category - business travel uses the standard Schengen Type C short-stay visa. An invitation letter from a Slovenian business entity may be required as supporting documentation. Fee EUR 90 adults, EUR 45 children 6-12. Must not involve remunerated employment in Slovenia.

visa_types.name: Single Permit (Combined Residence and Work Permit)
visa_types.purpose: Enables third-country nationals to enter, reside in, and work in Slovenia under a single unified administrative procedure, combining both the residence and work permit.
visa_types.notes: Issued for the duration of the employment contract, up to 2 years initially; first extension granted for up to 3 years. Employer must demonstrate no suitable EU/EEA worker was available (labour market test applies). A change of employer or employment terms requires written approval from the relevant administrative unit. Application submitted at Slovenian diplomatic mission abroad or administrative unit in Slovenia. Covers employees, self-employed (after 1 year legal residence), seasonal workers, intra-company transferees, posted workers, and trainees.

visa_types.name: EU Blue Card
visa_types.purpose: Allows highly qualified non-EU nationals to live and work in Slovenia in a highly paid, skilled position, with a streamlined path to intra-EU mobility and long-term residence.
visa_types.notes: Requires a valid employment contract or binding job offer for at least 6 months (amended 2025) with salary of at least 1.5x the average annual gross salary in Slovenia. Requires higher professional qualifications (university degree) or, for ICT professionals, demonstrated competence via certificates or expert opinion. Issued for the duration of the contract plus 3 months, max 2 years; renewable for up to 3 years. Initial application fee approximately EUR 70-102; residence permit card EUR 15.47. Grants enhanced intra-EU mobility rights after 12 months.

visa_types.name: Work Permit for Foreign Company Representative / Director
visa_types.purpose: Allows non-EU nationals who serve as directors, procurators, or authorised representatives of a Slovenian registered company to live and work in Slovenia.
visa_types.notes: For companies registered for less than 6 months: proof of minimum EUR 30,000 investment in company start-up is required. Companies with 10 or fewer workers may have no more than one such permit. Valid for up to 2 years; renewable. Application fee approximately EUR 90.

visa_types.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Digital Nomads
visa_types.purpose: Allows non-EU/EEA nationals who work remotely for employers or clients registered outside Slovenia to reside in Slovenia for up to one year without entering the Slovenian labour market.
visa_types.notes: Launched 21 November 2025. Applicant must have monthly funds of at least twice the average monthly net salary in Slovenia (approximately EUR 3,200/month as of early 2026). Must hold valid health insurance and have a clean criminal record. Work must be performed entirely via remote communication technologies for employers/clients outside Slovenia. Permit is not renewable; reapplication allowed 6 months after expiry. Family members may join immediately without duration restrictions. Applications submitted at Slovenian diplomatic missions abroad or any administrative unit in Slovenia.

visa_types.name: Long-Stay Visa (Type D) - Study / Temporary Residence Permit for Study
visa_types.purpose: Allows third-country nationals to enter Slovenia to pursue studies, vocational training, or other educational programmes at accredited institutions.
visa_types.notes: A Type D (national long-stay) visa authorises residence from 91 days up to 1 year and enables the holder to apply directly at an administrative unit in Slovenia for a temporary residence permit upon arrival. Students must provide proof of enrolment, financial means, accommodation, and health insurance. Fee EUR 90 adults (approx USD 98). After arriving, a formal temporary residence permit for study must be obtained from the administrative unit.

visa_types.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Family Reunification
visa_types.purpose: Permits third-country nationals who are family members of a legally residing non-EU national (or Slovenian citizen) to join and reside with their family member in Slovenia.
visa_types.notes: Eligible family members include spouses, registered partners, children under 21, dependent relatives, and long-term partners. Non-EU family members of EU/EEA citizens have a different and more favourable regime: they receive a residence permit for family members of EU citizens. Third-country national family members must apply at a Slovenian diplomatic mission abroad. Digital nomad permit holders may bring family members immediately without duration restrictions.

visa_types.name: Long-Stay National Visa (Type D)
visa_types.purpose: A national visa authorising third-country nationals to enter and reside in Slovenia for a period between 91 days and 1 year, issued for a specific purpose (work, study, family, etc.) before a full residence permit is granted.
visa_types.notes: The Type D visa serves as the entry document that enables a foreigner to enter Slovenia and then apply directly at an administrative unit for a temporary residence permit. It is purpose-specific (work, study, family reunification, etc.) and not a standalone long-term status. Fee EUR 90 for adults (approx USD 98). Processing normally 15 days, up to 45 days if additional examination needed. Applied for at Slovenian diplomatic missions abroad.

visa_types.name: Temporary Residence Permit via Business Investment (Company Director / Procurator)
visa_types.purpose: Grants residence and work rights to non-EU nationals who establish or invest in a Slovenian company and act as its director or authorised representative.
visa_types.notes: Slovenia has no standalone investor visa or golden visa program. Residence via investment flows from active business engagement (company director role). For companies registered less than 6 months: minimum investment of EUR 30,000 required as proof of company start-up capital. Permit valid up to 2 years, renewable. Path to permanent residence after 5 years continuous legal residence; citizenship available after 10 years total. Applications submitted at Slovenian diplomatic missions abroad or administrative units.
